National Science Foundation 0931843 (ActionWebs), Army Research Office W911NF-07-2-0019 (SCOS), Air Force Office of Scientific Research FA9550-06-0312 (HCDDES MURI), Air Force Research Lab, Multiscale Systems Center (MuSyC), Robert Bosch GmBH, Natio

e Systems Center (MuSyC), Robert Bosch GmBH, National Instruments, Thales and Toyota In the context of timed discrete-event systems, processes are allowed to realize functions that are not order-preserving with respect to the prefix ordering relation

rving with respect to the prefix ordering relation on the communicated sequences of values. This property renders naive applications of traditional domain-theoretic models inadequate for the semantic interpretation of such systems. Yet, interesting r

interpretation of such systems. Yet, interesting results have been obtained by imposing a fixed lower bound on the reaction time of the involved processes, effectively precluding Zeno behavior [1,2,3,4]. This work focuses on relaxing this requirement

4]. This work focuses on relaxing this requirement to obtain semantic interpretations even in the presence of Zeno conditions. The underlying aim is to establish a canonical denotational definition of timed discrete-event programming languages, there

timed discrete-event programming languages, thereby providing the means for reasoning about the correctness of the individual implementations, as well as allowing hidden commonalities of seemingly different timed systems to emerge. [1] R. K. Yates,

fferent timed systems to emerge. [1] R. K. Yates, "Networks of Real-Time Processes," CONCUR 93: Proc. Int. Conf. Concurrency Theory, Springer-Verlag, 1993, pp. 384-397. [2] E. A. Lee, " Modeling Concurrent Real-Time Processes Using Discrete Events ,"

rrent Real-Time Processes Using Discrete Events ," Annals of Software Engineering, Vol. 7, No. 3, 1999, pp. 25-45 (invited paper). [3] Xiaojun Liu, Edward A. Lee, " CPO Semantics of Timed Interactive Actor Networks ," EECS Department, University of C

Actor Networks ," EECS Department, University of California, Berkeley, Technical Report No. UCB/EECS-2007-131, November 5, 2007. [4] A. Cataldo, E. A. Lee, X. Liu, E. Matsikoudis and H. Zheng, " Discrete-Event Systems: Generalizing Metric Spaces and
ete-Event Systems: Generalizing Metric Spaces and Fixed Point Semantics ," UCB/ERL M05/12, April 8, 2005. Contact Update Help Copyright © 1999 - UC Regents; all rights reserved.
